title: Acute Pancreatitis
objectives: Apply diagnostic criteria for acute pancreatitis

vignette.text:
A 52-year-old woman presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to her back for 12 hours. Pain began after dinner, is constant and worsened by eating. She has nausea, vomiting, and reports fatty food intolerance and intermittent RUQ pain.

PMH: HTN, Obesity (BMI 34), T2DM, Hyperlipidemia
Meds: Lisinopril, Metformin, Atorvastatin
Social: No alcohol, no smoking
Allergies: Sulfa

physicalExam.abdomen: Epigastric tenderness with guarding, decreased bowel sounds

imaging.CT: Edematous pancreas with fat stranding, no necrosis

questions.question: Why does pancreatic pain radiate to the back?

options.text: Pancreas is retroperitoneal, lying against posterior abdominal wall and spine

questions.explanation: The pancreas lies retroperitoneally at L1-L2, anterior to spine. Inflammation directly irritates posterior structures and splanchnic nerves. Patients lean forward for relief.

sources.text: OpenStax A&P 2e
StatPearls: Pancreas Anatomy

questions.question: How does a gallstone cause pancreatitis anatomically?

options.text: CBD and pancreatic duct join at ampulla of Vater; stone impaction blocks both ducts

questions.explanation: The CBD and main pancreatic duct converge at the ampulla of Vater. A stone impacted there obstructs pancreatic drainage, causing duct hypertension and enzyme activation → pancreatitis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Gallstone Pancreatitis
OpenStax A&P

questions.stem: Decreased breath sounds at left base.
questions.question: Why do pleural effusions occur in pancreatitis, especially left-sided?

options.text: Inflammation tracks through diaphragmatic lymphatics; left-sided due to tail proximity

questions.explanation: Pleural effusions occur in 15-20% of pancreatitis. Left predominates due to pancreatic tail proximity to left hemidiaphragm. Fluid tracks via transdiaphragmatic lymphatics.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: Why is lipase preferred over amylase for diagnosis?

options.text: Lipase is more specific to pancreas and stays elevated longer (8-14 days)

questions.explanation: Lipase is more specific (mainly pancreas) and stays elevated 8-14 days vs amylase 3-5 days. Amylase also produced by salivary glands and elevated in other conditions.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Elevated BUN and hemoconcentration suggest dehydration.
questions.question: Why does pancreatitis cause significant fluid loss?

options.text: Massive inflammation increases capillary permeability, causing third-spacing

questions.explanation: Pancreatitis triggers massive inflammatory response with capillary leak. Fluid shifts into retroperitoneum and peritoneum. Patients can sequester 6-10+ liters, causing hemoconcentration and hypotension.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: Why does hypocalcemia occur in severe pancreatitis?

options.text: Saponification - calcium binds fatty acids from fat necrosis

questions.explanation: Lipase causes retroperitoneal fat necrosis, releasing fatty acids that bind calcium (saponification). Hypocalcemia is a severity marker (Ranson criteria).

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Gallstones and dilated CBD on imaging.
questions.question: What are the two most common causes of acute pancreatitis?
questions.explanation: Gallstones (40%) and alcohol (30%) cause ~70% of cases. Her elevated LFTs, dilated CBD, and stones indicate biliary etiology. GET SMASHED mnemonic covers other causes.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What lab pattern suggests gallstone vs alcoholic pancreatitis?

options.text: Gallstone: ALT >3× ULN has 95% PPV; Alcoholic: normal LFTs

questions.explanation: ALT >150 or >3× ULN has 95% PPV for gallstone etiology. Her pattern (elevated ALT, Alk Phos, bilirubin) indicates biliary cause. Guides management.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What distinguishes interstitial from necrotizing pancreatitis?

options.text: Interstitial: no necrosis (90%); Necrotizing: pancreatic/peripancreatic necrosis (10%)

questions.explanation: Interstitial (80-90%): inflammation without necrosis, usually mild. Necrotizing (10-20%): necrosis with higher morbidity, infection risk. CT contrast shows non-enhancing necrotic tissue.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the recommended fluid resuscitation?

options.text: LR 250-500 mL/hr initially with goal-directed adjustment

questions.explanation: Early aggressive fluids (first 12-24h) improve outcomes. LR preferred over NS. Rate: 250-500 mL/hr. Goals: HR <120, MAP >65, UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr. Avoid over-resuscitation.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is appropriate analgesia?

options.text: IV opioids (hydromorphone, fentanyl) are first-line; morphine acceptable

questions.explanation: IV opioids are first-line. Historical concern about morphine and sphincter of Oddi is not clinically supported. Adequate analgesia improves respiratory mechanics.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: Are prophylactic antibiotics indicated?

options.text: NO - not recommended; only for documented infection

questions.explanation: Prophylactic antibiotics NOT recommended - no benefit in preventing infected necrosis, may increase fungal infections. Only for documented infection.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: When is urgent ERCP indicated?

options.text: Concurrent cholangitis OR persistent biliary obstruction; not routine

questions.explanation: Urgent ERCP (<24h) for cholangitis or persistent obstruction. Most stones pass spontaneously. Routine early ERCP without cholangitis doesn't improve outcomes.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What are diagnostic criteria for acute pancreatitis?

options.text: 2 of 3: Characteristic pain, Lipase >3× ULN, Imaging findings

questions.explanation: Revised Atlanta: ≥2 of 3 criteria. She meets all 3: epigastric pain radiating to back, lipase >3× ULN (1842), CT findings. CT not always needed if clinical/lab clear.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What indicates severe acute pancreatitis?

options.text: Persistent organ failure (>48 hours): respiratory, cardiovascular, or renal

questions.explanation: Revised Atlanta severity: Mild (no organ failure), Moderately severe (transient organ failure <48h or local complications), Severe (persistent organ failure >48h). BISAP, Ranson, APACHE II help predict severity.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: When should feeding be initiated?

options.text: Early oral feeding within 24-48h if tolerated; enteral preferred over parenteral

questions.explanation: Early feeding (24-48h) improves outcomes. Start oral diet as tolerated. If unable, enteral nutrition (NG/NJ) preferred over TPN. Low-fat, soft diet initially. 'Pancreatic rest' is outdated.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: When should cholecystectomy be performed for gallstone pancreatitis?

options.text: Same hospitalization (ideally within 72h) once pancreatitis improves

questions.explanation: Cholecystectomy during same admission reduces recurrence (30% at 6 weeks if delayed). Ideally within 72 hours once pancreatitis improves. Delay increases recurrent attacks.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: On day 7, she develops fever and worsening pain.
questions.question: What complication should be suspected?

options.text: Infected pancreatic necrosis - requires CT-guided FNA for diagnosis

questions.explanation: Infected necrosis typically develops after week 1. Suspect with new fever, leukocytosis, clinical decline. CT may show gas in necrosis. FNA confirms bacteria. Requires antibiotics and often drainage/necrosectomy.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: CT at 4 weeks shows a walled-off collection.
questions.question: What is a walled-off necrosis (WON)?

options.text: Mature collection with wall containing necrotic tissue; develops >4 weeks

questions.explanation: WON: encapsulated necrotic collection with defined wall (>4 weeks). Differs from pseudocyst (fluid only, no solid). May be sterile or infected. Management depends on symptoms/infection.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: She develops sudden hypotension and distension.
questions.question: What vascular complication can occur?

options.text: Splenic artery pseudoaneurysm rupture or splenic vein thrombosis

questions.explanation: Pancreatic enzymes can erode vessels causing pseudoaneurysm (splenic, gastroduodenal arteries). Rupture causes massive hemorrhage. Splenic vein thrombosis causes left-sided portal HTN with gastric varices.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: Which BEST synthesizes this presentation?

options.text: Gallstone pancreatitis (interstitial/edematous) requiring aggressive fluids, pain control, and same-admission cholecystectomy

questions.explanation: Synthesis: Gallstone etiology (stones, elevated LFTs, dilated CBD), interstitial type (no necrosis on CT), moderately severe (SIRS present). Plan: aggressive LR, analgesia, early feeding, same-admission cholecystectomy.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the comprehensive plan?

options.text: Aggressive fluids, pain control, early nutrition, monitor for complications, cholecystectomy this admission

questions.explanation: Comprehensive: (1) Aggressive LR resuscitation; (2) IV opioid analgesia; (3) Early oral diet when tolerated; (4) No prophylactic antibiotics; (5) Monitor for organ failure/complications; (6) Cholecystectomy during same admission once improved.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
